nativeRange.habitat: Small, slow-moving to moderately flowing streams, swamps, and floodplain pools; typically in shallow, heavily vegetated areas with sandy or muddy substrates and leaf litter
nativeRange.biotope: African savanna stream / floodplain pool with leaf litter and marginal vegetation

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Anubias, floating plants)
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves) for biotope authenticity and tannin release
driftwood roots and branches for cover

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of small foods: high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and frozen or live foods including brine shrimp nauplii, daphnia, micro worms, and bloodworm (chopped). Vegetable matter (spirulina flake, blanched spinach) rounds out the diet. Small mouth size means particle size should be appropriate; large pellets are refused. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily.

goodWith: other small peaceful African fish (Congo tetras at similar size, Phenacogrammus tetras)
dwarf cichlids that tolerate soft water (Nanochromis, Pelvicachromis at cautious pairing)
Corydoras species preferring soft acidic water
small peaceful rasboras and danios of similar size
avoidWith: boisterous or fin-nipping barbs (Tiger Barbs) that may outcompete
predatory fish that can swallow 5–6 cm fish
African Red Eye Tetra (Arnoldichthys spilopterus) — commonly confused with this species but belongs to family Alestidae and has different care requirements

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Enteromius fasciolatus; verify availability and attribution before use.

summary: A small, lively shoaling barb from Angola, DR Congo, and Zambia, reaching ~6 cm. Named for its attractive dark vertical banding pattern. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water and groups of 6+. Peaceful; suitable for community aquaria with similarly sized softwater species.
careNotes: The African Banded Barb (Enteromius fasciolatus) is a small, active cyprinid best kept in groups of six or more, where shoaling behaviour and natural colouration are most pronounced. Lone individuals or pairs tend to be shy and stressed. A tank of at least 80 L with a 75 cm footprint is recommended to provide adequate horizontal swimming room. Water quality is important: this species originates from soft, slightly acidic blackwater-influenced streams and floodplain pools in Angola, DR Congo, and Zambia. Aim for pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12, and temperature 20–26°C. Tannin-stained water achieved with Indian almond leaves or driftwood is appreciated and may enhance colouration. Avoid hard, alkaline water, which leads to long-term health decline. Filtration should provide gentle to moderate flow; avoid strong currents. The species is often confused with superficially similar small African barbs and, incorrectly, with the African Red Eye Tetra (Arnoldichthys spilopterus, family Alestidae), which is a wholly unrelated characid. Taxonomically, the genus was revised from Barbus to Enteromius following Berrebi and Tsigenopoulos genus-level revisions of African cyprinids; older literature and trade sources frequently still use Barbus fasciolatus. The species is not commonly encountered in the hobby and may require specialist sources.
breedingNotes: Spawning in captivity is possible but uncommonly reported. Like other small barbs, E. fasciolatus is an egg-scatterer with no parental care. A dedicated breeding tank with fine-leaved plants (Java moss, spawning mops) or a mesh bottom to protect eggs from predation is recommended. Slightly raising the temperature to 24–26°C and conditioning both sexes with live and frozen foods can trigger spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–48 hours; fry require infusoria or commercial fry food initially, graduating to brine shrimp nauplii. Remove adults after spawning to prevent egg predation.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Enteromius fasciolatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Angola, DR Congo, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Botswana), water parameters (temperature 20–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm as dH equivalents), adult size (~6 cm), habitat description (slow streams, swamps, leaf litter), diet, grouping, and taxonomic notes on the Barbus-to-Enteromius reclassification.

additional.name: FishBase — Enteromius fasciolatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), adult size (to 6.0 cm TL), native range countries, and temperature tolerance. FishBase may still index under Barbus fasciolatus as a synonym redirect.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — African barb species profiles
additional.notes: Background on African barb diversity, habitat types, and general care guidance for softwater African cyprinids including Enteromius-group barbs. Used to corroborate soft-acid water preferences and shoaling behaviour notes.

additional.name: Berrebi P. & Tsigenopoulos C.S. — Phylogeny and taxonomy of African Barbus sensu lato (Cyprinidae)
additional.notes: Underpins the genus-level reclassification of Barbus fasciolatus to Enteromius fasciolatus. Key taxonomic reference for the alsoKnownAs old-genus note.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Barb Care Guides
additional.notes: General barb husbandry guidance used to supplement tank size, grouping, and diet recommendations. No dedicated African Banded Barb page found; general barb guidance applied where consistent with species-specific sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer with no parental care. Breeding possible in dedicated soft-acid tanks with fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Condition with live/frozen foods; raise temperature to 24–26°C. Remove adults post-spawn; fry require infusoria then brine shrimp nauplii.
